repository_ssim: St. Catherine's Monastery of the Sinai
shelfmark_ssi: UTO Sinai Arabic NF M 66, SPP No. 337
contents_tesim: Previously unknown hexameters of mythological content on the childhood of Dionysus. The presence of proparoxytone words at the end of the verses, of spondaic verses, and of consecutive spondees rules out the attribution of this work to a follower of the Nonnus of Panopolis school and of his "modern style." The style is rather archaic, as in the uninterrupted reuse of Homeric formulae at the beginning and end of the verses
full_text_tesim: Previously unknown hexameters of mythological content on the childhood of Dionysus. The presence of proparoxytone words at the end of the verses, of spondaic verses, and of consecutive spondees rules out the attribution of this work to a follower of the Nonnus of Panopolis school and of his "modern style." The style is rather archaic, as in the uninterrupted reuse of Homeric formulae at the beginning and end of the verses

This text comes from a luxurious manuscript, whose folios measured approximately 300 x 250 mm (Turner group II). The letters are 3 mm high and 2-4 mm wide, the space between the lines is 7-8 mm. The margins are large: the internal margin measures 30 mm, the external margin 50 mm, the upper margin 40 mm (the lower margin is not preserved). The written surface is 175 x 155 mm. At the end of the lines the letters are smaller, in order to respect the line division and the justification of the margins, which is a feature typical of luxury codices
UTO Sinai Arabic NF M 66, SPP No. 337

Very small Alexandrian majuscule with influences of Biblical majuscule (in the letters mu, pi, omega). Critical signs, diacritics, and interlinear corrections were added by a later hand (7th-8th c. CE?)
